# Preface {.unnumbered}

Medicine has always had two jobs: to **treat disease**, and to **protect health**. The
second is easy to neglect — it is quieter, slower, and rarely an emergency — yet it is
where most of today's gains in length and quality of life are won. *Health in Medicine* is
about that second job: the everyday clinical work of helping people stay well. And no
single lever matters more to it than **nutrition**.

In Singapore, the commitment to keeping people well — not just treating them once they are
sick — now has a national plan. **Healthier SG**, launched on 5 July 2023 after its 2022
White Paper to Parliament, set out to re-orient the health system *"from reactively caring
for those who are already sick, to proactively preventing individuals from falling ill"*
[@mohHealthierSG]. Every resident is invited to enrol with a family doctor and build a
personalised health plan around diet, activity, screening and sleep. This book teaches the
part of that plan that sits at the centre of all the rest: **what, and how, people eat.**

## Why a whole book on nutrition

Because the numbers demand it. Non-communicable diseases — heart disease, diabetes,
cancers, chronic lung disease — now cause around **three-quarters of all deaths worldwide**
[@who2020diet]. They are, in large part, diseases of how we live, and **diet is among the
single largest modifiable drivers** [@gbd2019diet]. Close to home, **one in three
Singaporeans** has a lifetime risk of diabetes [@mohWarOnDiabetes] — yet the National
Nutrition Survey 2022 found only about **4% of the staples we eat are wholegrain**, **nine
in ten** of us exceed the sodium target, and saturated fat makes up a large share of our
fat intake [@mohNNS2022].

Here is the paradox this book exists to fix. Diet is one of the most powerful levers a
doctor has — and one of the **least taught** subjects in medical training. A 2019 review
across five world regions concluded bluntly that *"nutrition is insufficiently incorporated
into medical education, regardless of country, setting, or year of medical education"*
[@crowley2019]. Singapore is taking the opposite bet: from **2026**, NUS Medicine is
expanding graduate training in **nutrition and preventive health**, built around Asia's
ageing population and rising chronic disease. You are learning this at exactly the moment
the field is being built around you.

## What this book is

*Health in Medicine* is an **interactive companion** to your first-year nutrition and
metabolism teaching. It is **Singapore-first** — anchored in the tools you already live
with: **My Healthy Plate** ("quarter, quarter, half"), **Nutri-Grade** on your *kopi* and
bubble tea, the **War on Diabetes** — before generalising to the international evidence.

It is built for an integrated study loop:

> **read → explore → self-test → rehearse**

Each chapter pairs concise text with live diagrams, **self-assessment quizzes** that give
instant feedback, and **branching clinical cases** in which you practise advising real
patients — a *cai png* lunch, a high screening glucose, a tired young woman with heavy
periods. All progress is saved **locally in your browser**; there is no account and no
login.

## How to read it

- The chapters build on each other. **Part I** lays a firm metabolic and physiological
  foundation; later parts apply it to advising well people, then to common conditions, then
  to integrated practice.
- Treat the **quizzes and cases as the point**, not the garnish. Counselling is a skill,
  and skills are built by doing.
- Notice the **honesty about evidence**. Throughout, landmark trials appear *with* their
  caveats — a relative risk on a composite endpoint is not the same as "prevents heart
  attacks." Learning to read a claim carefully is itself a clinical competency.

## What it will, and won't, do

This year is about **motivation, concepts, and the clinical conversation**. It will build
your nutritional literacy and your confidence to advise. It will **not** turn you into a
dietitian or hand you prescribing protocols — detailed medical nutrition therapy and
disease management come later, and you will learn *when to refer*.

A doctor who can help a patient eat well is practising medicine at its most preventive and
most human. That work starts here.

::: {.callout-note}
## A note for this pilot
This is an early build demonstrating the format with a sample of Part I chapters. Content,
cases and citations are under active development and review; figures are quoted with their
sources so they can be checked.
:::
